title: Simple basic statistics concepts
text:
Welcome
This course teaches statistics the way research actually works starting with your data, not a textbook formula. Every method is introduced with a practical scenario, explained in plain language, and then implemented step-by-step in R.
The course is designed for PhD students in biomedical and clinical research. That includes people working with human patient data, animal experiments, survey data, or longitudinal studies. You do not need to be an expert in R or statistics to start, but you do need R and RStudio installed on your computer.

title: Simple basic statistics concepts
text:
How the Course Is Organized
The course has four parts. Each part builds on the previous one.






TipChoose Your Starting Point










Your background
Where to start




New to statistics
Part 1 work through the course in order


Familiar with R, new to modeling
Part 1 then continue to Part 2


Interested in regression and prediction
Part 2 after reviewing Part 1 if needed


Looking for advanced methods only
Part 3 after the core concepts in Parts 1 and 2


Need a quick method guide
Part 4 for the reference and resources



If you are unsure where to begin, start with Part 1 and follow the course sequentially.








TipYour Quick Reference



The Statistical Decision Guide in Part 4 is a reference card you can return to at any point. It tells you which method to use for common research questions and links back to the relevant sessions.



Part 1: Inference — Testing and Estimation
This part introduces the foundations of statistical inference and hypothesis testing. You will learn how to interpret p-values, confidence intervals, and effect sizes before moving on to regression and modeling.

Sampling, confidence intervals, and the central limit theorem
Hypothesis testing and the logic of evidence
t-tests for comparing means
Non-parametric alternatives when assumptions fail
ANOVA for comparing multiple groups
Categorical tests such as chi-square and Fisher’s exact
Power and sample size planning



Part 2: Regression and Modeling
This part covers the most commonly used predictive models in biomedical research. You will learn how to fit models, interpret coefficients, check assumptions, and report results clearly.

Correlation and association
Linear regression for continuous outcomes
Multiple regression for adjusted effects
Logistic regression for binary outcomes
Model building, diagnostics, and validation
Missing data handling with multiple imputation
Mixed models for repeated measures and clustered data



Part 3: Advanced Methods
This part introduces advanced analytic methods for more complex study designs and causal questions.

Survival analysis and time-to-event data
Causal inference using DAGs and observational data
Mendelian randomization for genetic instrumental variables



Part 4: Reference and Resources
This part gives you tools to apply the methods thoughtfully and find answers when you need them.

Statistical Decision Guide for method selection
Clinical research methods and study design considerations
Help and documentation for using the course materials

title: Simple basic statistics concepts
text:
Prerequisites

R and RStudio installed on your computer
Basic ability to run R code in RStudio
No prior statistics knowledge is assumed beyond high-school level

title: Simple basic statistics concepts
text:
How to Use This Course
Each session follows the same structure:

When do you use this? A concrete scenario from your field
Background Why the method works, in plain language
Worked examples Step-by-step R code on real data
What can go wrong Common mistakes and how to avoid them
Exercises Practice problems with solutions
Comprehension check Self-check questions

You can work through the course week by week, or jump to a specific method when you need it. If you are unsure which method to use, go to the Statistical Decision Guide in Part 4.

title: 1  Before You Begin: Setup
text:
1.1 Step 1: Install R and RStudio
This page tells you everything you need to do once, before starting the course. The setup takes about 10-15 minutes. After that, each session just works.
You need two things on your computer:
Install R first, then RStudio. On Windows, accept all defaults during installation.
To check your R version, run this in the RStudio Console:
R.version.string

title: 1  Before You Begin: Setup
text:
Software
What it is
Download




R (version = 4.2)
The statistical computing language
cran.r-project.org


RStudio Desktop
The editor you will use to write and run R code
posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop

title: 1  Before You Begin: Setup
text:
Step 1b: Install Rtools (Windows Only)






ImportantWindows users must do this before installing packages



Many R packages compile C or Fortran code during installation. Without Rtools, Windows will silently install outdated pre-compiled versions of packages, which can cause version conflicts that break Quarto rendering later.

Go to cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/Rtools/
Download and install the version that matches your R version (e.g., Rtools44 for R 4.4, Rtools43 for R 4.3)
Accept all defaults (no changes needed)
Restart RStudio after installation

To confirm Rtools is active, run this in the Console; you should see TRUE:
pkgbuild::has_build_tools(debug = TRUE)

title: 1  Before You Begin: Setup
text:
1.2 Step 2: Install Course Packages (CRAN)
Copy and paste the block below into your RStudio Console and press Enter. This installs every package used across all sessions in one go. You only need to do this once.






WarningThis will take 10-20 minutes (that is normal)



Installing 25+ packages for the first time takes time. R will print a lot of output as it downloads and compiles each package. Do not close RStudio and do not interrupt the process. Just wait until you see the > prompt return in the Console. Go make a coffee.



install.packages(c(
  # Quarto rendering essentials (must have current versions)
  "knitr",
  "rmarkdown",
  "xfun",

  # Core data wrangling and visualization (used in every session)
  "tidyverse",
  "patchwork",

  # Exploration and summary tables
  "skimr",
  "gtsummary",
  "GGally",
  "nhanesA",

  # Data import
  "readxl",
  "palmerpenguins",

  # Missing data
  "naniar",
  "mice",

  # Survival analysis
  "survival",
  "ggsurvfit",

  # Regression and modeling
  "broom",
  "broom.mixed",
  "car",
  "lme4",
  "glmnet",
  "parsnip",
  "performance",
  "pROC",
  "AER",

  # Causal inference
  "dagitty",
  "ggdag",

  # Inference and testing
  "pwr",

  # Reproducibility
  "reprex"
))


# Install Bioconductor packages used in some sessions
if (!requireNamespace("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE)) install.packages("BiocManager")
## (Omics session removed)
## Bioconductor note: advanced visualization only







TipIf a package fails to install



Run install.packages("packagename") individually to see the specific error message. On Windows, restart RStudio and try again; some packages need RTools. On macOS, you may need to install Xcode Command Line Tools first: xcode-select --install in the Terminal.

title: 1  Before You Begin: Setup
text:
1.3 Step 3: Install Bioconductor Packages
The Advanced Visualization session optionally uses a Bioconductor package. Install it with:

if (!requireNamespace("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE))
  install.packages("BiocManager")

BiocManager::install(c(
  "GEOquery"        # fetching GEO datasets (advanced-visualization session)
))







WarningWhen BiocManager asks about updates, press a



During installation you may see:
Update all/some/none? [a/s/n]:
Type a and press Enter. This updates all dependent packages to compatible versions. Pressing n can leave you with mismatched package versions that cause errors later.








Note



Bioconductor packages can take 5-10 minutes to install. If you are not doing the Advanced Visualization session, you can skip this step.

remotes::install_github("MRCIEU/TwoSampleMR")

This requires an internet connection. If you are not doing the Mendelian Randomization session, you can skip this step.

title: 1  Before You Begin: Setup
text:
1.5 Step 5: Verify Your Setup
Run this block to confirm the core packages are installed. You should see no errors.

core_pkgs <- c(
  "knitr", "rmarkdown", "xfun",
  "tidyverse", "patchwork", "skimr", "gtsummary", "broom",
  "survival", "ggsurvfit", "lme4", "broom.mixed", "car",
  "glmnet", "performance", "pROC", "mice", "naniar",
  "pwr", "dagitty", "ggdag", "GGally", "nhanesA",
  "palmerpenguins", "AER", "reprex"
)

missing <- core_pkgs[!sapply(core_pkgs, requireNamespace, quietly = TRUE)]

if (length(missing) == 0) {
  message("All core packages are installed. You are ready to start.")
} else {
  message("Missing packages: ", paste(missing, collapse = ", "))
  message("Run: install.packages(c(", paste0('"', missing, '"', collapse = ", "), "))")
}

title: 2  Sampling, Estimation, and Confidence Intervals
text: 2.1 When Do You Use This?
crumbs: 2  Sampling, Estimation, and Confidence Intervals

title: 2  Sampling, Estimation, and Confidence Intervals
text: You have collected a sample and want to make inferences about the broader population it came from, not just describe the sample itself. Estimation produces a best guess (point estimate) for a population quantity along with a confidence interval that quantifies how uncertain that guess is given your sample size. For example: estimating the mean level of a biomarker in all patients with a condition from a clinic sample, or the proportion who respond to treatment in a trial.
crumbs: 2  Sampling, Estimation, and Confidence Intervals

title: 2  Sampling, Estimation, and Confidence Intervals
text:
2.2 Learning Objectives
After completing this session you will be able to:

Explain what a sampling distribution and standard error are
State the Central Limit Theorem and explain why it matters for inference
Compute confidence intervals for means and proportions in R
Interpret a 95% confidence interval correctly
Distinguish between a confidence interval and a prediction interval
Use bootstrap resampling when distributional assumptions are uncertain
crumbs: 2  Sampling, Estimation, and Confidence Intervals

title: 2  Sampling, Estimation, and Confidence Intervals
text:
2.3 Background: From Sample to Population
You can never measure every person, mouse, or cell. You measure a sample and use it to estimate a population quantity. Three ideas underpin all of this:
Point estimate: Your best single guess at the population value (e.g., the sample mean).
Standard error (SE): How much your point estimate would vary if you drew many different samples of the same size. Smaller SE = more precise estimate. SE shrinks as sample size grows.
Confidence interval (CI): A range of plausible values for the population parameter, constructed so that (in repeated sampling) the specified percentage of intervals would contain the true value.
\[SE(\bar{x}) = \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}\]
\[95\% \text{ CI for } \mu \approx \bar{x} \pm 1.96 \times SE(\bar{x})\]

2.3.1 The Central Limit Theorem
The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) states: regardless of the shape of the original distribution, the sampling distribution of the mean becomes approximately normal as sample size increases.
This is why t-tests and confidence intervals work even when individual measurements are not normally distributed - as long as the sample size is large enough (roughly \(n \geq 30\) for mildly skewed data; larger for heavily skewed data).
crumbs: 2  Sampling, Estimation, and Confidence Intervals

title: 2  Sampling, Estimation, and Confidence Intervals
text:
2.4 Example 1: Clinical Data (PBC)

pbc <- survival::pbc %>%
  as_tibble() %>%
  janitor::clean_names() %>%
  mutate(
    sex   = factor(sex, levels = c("f", "m"), labels = c("Female", "Male")),
    trt   = factor(trt, levels = c(1, 2),     labels = c("D-penicillamine", "Placebo"))
  )


2.4.1 Estimating Mean Albumin

albumin_est <- pbc %>%
  filter(!is.na(albumin)) %>%
  summarise(
    n        = n(),
    mean     = mean(albumin),
    sd       = sd(albumin),
    se       = sd / sqrt(n),
    ci_lower = mean - qt(0.975, df = n - 1) * se,
    ci_upper = mean + qt(0.975, df = n - 1) * se
  )
albumin_est

# A tibble: 1 × 6
      n  mean    sd     se ci_lower ci_upper
  <int> <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>
1   418  3.50 0.425 0.0208     3.46     3.54


Interpretation: We estimate the mean serum albumin to be 3.5 g/dL (95% CI: 3.46 to 3.54). If we were to repeat this study 100 times with different samples of the same size, approximately 95 of the resulting intervals would contain the true population mean.


2.4.2 Quicker with t.test()

t.test(pbc$albumin)


    One Sample t-test

data:  pbc$albumin
t = 168.26, df = 417, p-value < 2.2e-16
alternative hypothesis: true mean is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 3.456582 3.538299
sample estimates:
mean of x 
  3.49744 


t.test() on a single vector returns the 95% CI for the mean without needing a hypothesis (the default null \(\mu = 0\) is irrelevant here; we only want the interval).


2.4.3 Confidence Interval for a Proportion
What proportion of patients are female?

n_female <- sum(pbc$sex == "Female", na.rm = TRUE)
n_total  <- sum(!is.na(pbc$sex))

prop.test(x = n_female, n = n_total, conf.level = 0.95)


    1-sample proportions test with continuity correction

data:  n_female out of n_total, null probability 0.5
X-squared = 258.95, df = 1, p-value < 2.2e-16
alternative hypothesis: true p is not equal to 0.5
95 percent confidence interval:
 0.8603074 0.9216882
sample estimates:
        p 
0.8947368 


The Wilson interval from prop.test() is better than the simple Wald interval for proportions, especially when the proportion is near 0 or 1.


2.4.4 Comparing Estimates Across Groups

pbc %>%
  filter(!is.na(trt), !is.na(albumin)) %>%
  group_by(trt) %>%
  summarise(
    n        = n(),
    mean     = mean(albumin),
    se       = sd(albumin) / sqrt(n),
    ci_lower = mean - qt(0.975, df = n - 1) * se,
    ci_upper = mean + qt(0.975, df = n - 1) * se
  )

# A tibble: 2 × 6
  trt                 n  mean     se ci_lower ci_upper
  <fct>           <int> <dbl>  <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>
1 D-penicillamine   158  3.52 0.0353     3.45     3.59
2 Placebo           154  3.52 0.0319     3.46     3.59




2.4.5 Visualising the Estimates

pbc %>%
  filter(!is.na(trt), !is.na(albumin)) %>%
  group_by(trt) %>%
  summarise(
    n        = n(),
    mean     = mean(albumin),
    se       = sd(albumin) / sqrt(n),
    ci_lower = mean - qt(0.975, df = n - 1) * se,
    ci_upper = mean + qt(0.975, df = n - 1) * se
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = trt, y = mean, ymin = ci_lower, ymax = ci_upper)) +
  geom_point(size = 4, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_errorbar(width = 0.1, color = "steelblue", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Mean serum albumin by treatment group",
    subtitle = "Points = mean; error bars = 95% CI",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Albumin (g/dL)"
  ) +
  theme_bw()
crumbs: 2  Sampling, Estimation, and Confidence Intervals

title: 2  Sampling, Estimation, and Confidence Intervals
text:
2.5 Example 2: Animal Data (Mouse Weights)
Suppose we measure body weight in a small experiment: 20 mice in each of two treatment groups.

set.seed(2024)
mice <- tibble(
  group  = rep(c("Control", "Treatment"), each = 20),
  weight = c(rnorm(20, mean = 28, sd = 3),
             rnorm(20, mean = 25, sd = 3))
)

mice %>%
  group_by(group) %>%
  summarise(n = n(), mean = mean(weight), sd = sd(weight),
            se = sd / sqrt(n),
            ci_lower = mean - qt(0.975, df = n - 1) * se,
            ci_upper = mean + qt(0.975, df = n - 1) * se)

# A tibble: 2 × 7
  group         n  mean    sd    se ci_lower ci_upper
  <chr>     <int> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>
1 Control      20  27.1  3.63 0.811     25.4     28.8
2 Treatment    20  24.7  2.64 0.591     23.5     25.9



2.5.1 Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
When the distribution is non-normal or the sample is small, bootstrap CIs are more reliable. The boot package resamples the data with replacement many times to build an empirical sampling distribution.

# Bootstrap 95% CI for mean weight in control mice
ctrl_weights <- mice$weight[mice$group == "Control"]

boot_mean <- boot(data  = ctrl_weights,
                  statistic = function(x, i) mean(x[i]),
                  R     = 1000)

boot.ci(boot_mean, type = "perc")

BOOTSTRAP CONFIDENCE INTERVAL CALCULATIONS
Based on 1000 bootstrap replicates

CALL : 
boot.ci(boot.out = boot_mean, type = "perc")

Intervals : 
Level     Percentile     
95%   (25.47, 28.59 )  
Calculations and Intervals on Original Scale


The percentile bootstrap CI takes the 2.5th and 97.5th percentile of the bootstrapped means as the interval bounds - no distributional assumptions required.
crumbs: 2  Sampling, Estimation, and Confidence Intervals

title: 2  Sampling, Estimation, and Confidence Intervals
section: 2.6 Where to Find Data Like This
text:
2.6 Where to Find Data Like This
PBC dataset: survival::pbc - built in, no download needed.
Simulating data for teaching:

# Balanced two-group experiment
set.seed(42)
dat <- tibble(
  group  = rep(c("A", "B"), each = 30),
  value  = c(rnorm(30, mean = 10, sd = 2),
             rnorm(30, mean = 12, sd = 2))
)

Boot package datasets: boot::melanoma, boot::aircondit and others contain real datasets useful for bootstrap demonstrations.
crumbs: 2  Sampling, Estimation, and Confidence Intervals

title: 2  Sampling, Estimation, and Confidence Intervals
text:
2.7 What Can Go Wrong
Misinterpreting a confidence interval A 95% CI does not mean “there is a 95% probability the true mean lies in this interval.” The true mean either is or is not in the interval - it is not random. The correct interpretation is: “if we repeated the study many times, 95% of such intervals would contain the true mean.”
Using a z-interval when n is small The formula \(\bar{x} \pm 1.96 \times SE\) uses the normal quantile 1.96. For small samples, use the t-distribution quantile: qt(0.975, df = n - 1). t.test() does this automatically.
Ignoring missing data If you compute mean(x) without na.rm = TRUE, R returns NA. Worse, if missing values are not random (e.g., sicker patients more likely to have missing labs), your estimate will be biased.
Treating overlapping CIs as proof of no difference Non-overlapping CIs do imply a significant difference (at approximately p < 0.05). But overlapping CIs do not prove no difference - you need a proper two-sample test. The 95% CI for each group mean is not the same as the 95% CI for their difference.
crumbs: 2  Sampling, Estimation, and Confidence Intervals

title: 2  Sampling, Estimation, and Confidence Intervals
text:
2.8 Exercises

2.8.1 Exercise 1 (Guided): CI for Bilirubin
Using survival::pbc:

Compute the sample mean, SD, SE, and 95% CI for serum bilirubin (bili).
Bilirubin is right-skewed. Repeat using log(bili). Report the geometric mean by back-transforming with exp().
Compare the raw and geometric means. Which better represents the “typical” patient?







TipExercise 1: Solution






# Raw bilirubin
pbc %>%
  filter(!is.na(bili)) %>%
  summarise(
    n        = n(),
    mean     = mean(bili),
    se       = sd(bili) / sqrt(n),
    ci_lower = mean - qt(0.975, df = n - 1) * se,
    ci_upper = mean + qt(0.975, df = n - 1) * se
  )

# A tibble: 1 × 5
      n  mean    se ci_lower ci_upper
  <int> <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>
1   418  3.22 0.216     2.80     3.64

# Log-transformed
log_est <- pbc %>%
  filter(!is.na(bili)) %>%
  summarise(
    n        = n(),
    log_mean = mean(log(bili)),
    se       = sd(log(bili)) / sqrt(n),
    ci_lower = log_mean - qt(0.975, df = n - 1) * se,
    ci_upper = log_mean + qt(0.975, df = n - 1) * se
  )

# Back-transform to original scale
log_est %>%
  mutate(across(c(log_mean, ci_lower, ci_upper), exp))

# A tibble: 1 × 5
      n log_mean     se ci_lower ci_upper
  <int>    <dbl>  <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>
1   418     1.77 0.0501     1.60     1.95


The geometric mean is lower than the arithmetic mean, because the arithmetic mean is pulled upward by extreme values. The geometric mean better represents the median patient in a right-skewed distribution.





2.8.2 Exercise 2 (Semi-guided): Bootstrap vs t-interval
Using the mouse weight data created above (or simulate your own 15 observations from a skewed distribution):

Compute the t-interval for the mean using the formula and verify it matches t.test().
Compute a bootstrap percentile CI using boot().
Are the two intervals similar? If the distribution were more skewed, which would you trust more?







TipExercise 2: Solution






set.seed(2024)
weights <- rnorm(15, mean = 28, sd = 4)

# t-interval
n <- length(weights)
m <- mean(weights); s <- sd(weights)
ci_t <- c(m - qt(0.975, df = n-1) * s/sqrt(n),
          m + qt(0.975, df = n-1) * s/sqrt(n))
ci_t

[1] 24.79043 30.29735

t.test(weights)$conf.int

[1] 24.79043 30.29735
attr(,"conf.level")
[1] 0.95

# bootstrap
library(boot)
b <- boot(weights, function(x, i) mean(x[i]), R = 2000)
boot.ci(b, type = "perc")$percent[4:5]

[1] 25.03384 29.76708


For normal data, both intervals are similar. For skewed data or small samples, the bootstrap is more reliable because it makes no normality assumption.





2.8.3 Exercise 3 (Open-ended)
Using a dataset from your own research, compute 95% confidence intervals for the key outcome variable, separately by group. Produce an error-bar plot. Write a one-paragraph interpretation as you would for a Results section.
crumbs: 2  Sampling, Estimation, and Confidence Intervals

title: 2  Sampling, Estimation, and Confidence Intervals
text:
2.9 Comprehension Check

What is the standard error of the mean, and how does it change as sample size increases?
A study reports: “mean albumin 3.5 g/dL (95% CI: 3.3 to 3.7).” What does this mean?
Why does the Central Limit Theorem matter for confidence intervals even when the data are not normally distributed?
You have a sample of 10 mice with a skewed weight distribution. Should you use a t-interval or a bootstrap CI? Why?
Two groups have CIs of [10.0, 12.0] and [11.5, 13.5]. They overlap at [11.5, 12.0]. Can you conclude there is no significant difference between the groups?







NoteAnswers






The standard error is \(SE = s / \sqrt{n}\), where \(s\) is the sample standard deviation. It measures how precisely the sample mean estimates the population mean. SE decreases as \(n\) increases (as \(1/\sqrt{n}\)) — doubling the sample size reduces the SE by a factor of \(\sqrt{2} \approx 1.41\).
Our best estimate of the mean population albumin is 3.5 g/dL. If we repeated this study many times with samples of the same size, 95% of the resulting confidence intervals would contain the true population mean.
The CLT guarantees that for sufficiently large samples, the sampling distribution of the mean is approximately normal regardless of the original distribution shape. This allows us to use the normal (or t) distribution to construct valid CIs even for skewed data.
With only 10 mice and skewed data, the normality assumption underlying the t-interval is suspect. Use a bootstrap CI, which makes no distributional assumption.
No. Overlapping CIs do not imply no significant difference. The CI for each group mean is not the CI for their difference. You need a two-sample t-test (or equivalent) to formally test whether the groups differ.
crumbs: 2  Sampling, Estimation, and Confidence Intervals

title: 2  Sampling, Estimation, and Confidence Intervals
text:
2.10 How to Report






NoteReporting Estimates and Confidence Intervals



In a Methods section:

“Results are reported as mean (95% confidence interval) unless otherwise stated. Bootstrap confidence intervals (1,000 resamples) were used for [statistic] because [reason].”

In a Results section:

“The mean [outcome] was X.XX (95% CI: L.LL to U.UU). The estimated [proportion/rate] was X.XX% (95% CI: L.LL% to U.UU%).”

Always report: - The point estimate and its 95% CI (not just the estimate alone) - Whether the CI is a normal-theory CI, bootstrap CI, or from a model - Sample size n so readers can judge precision
Common reporting errors: - Quoting ± (standard deviation) when you mean ± (standard error) - they answer different questions - Saying “the true value is in this interval” - a 95% CI means 95% of intervals constructed this way contain the true value - Reporting a CI without a point estimate
crumbs: 2  Sampling, Estimation, and Confidence Intervals

title: 2  Sampling, Estimation, and Confidence Intervals
text:
2.11 Further Reading

R for Data Science — Wickham & Grolemund
Introduction to the Bootstrap — Efron & Tibshirani (1993)
Bland (2015) — Statistics textbook written for clinical researchers





Bland, Martin. 2015. An Introduction to Medical Statistics. 4th ed. Oxford University Press.
crumbs: 2  Sampling, Estimation, and Confidence Intervals

title: 3  Hypothesis Testing: Concepts and Framework
text: 3.1 When Do You Use This?
crumbs: 3  Hypothesis Testing: Concepts and Framework

title: 3  Hypothesis Testing: Concepts and Framework
text: You have two groups of patients and you want to know whether they differ on a lab value. Or you have a new treatment and you want to know whether it works better than placebo. Hypothesis testing gives you a principled framework for making decisions from data while controlling the rate of false positives.
crumbs: 3  Hypothesis Testing: Concepts and Framework

title: 3  Hypothesis Testing: Concepts and Framework
text:
3.2 Learning Objectives
After completing this session you will be able to:

State null and alternative hypotheses for a comparison
Explain what a p-value is and what it is not
Describe Type I and Type II errors and how they relate to \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\)
Explain statistical power and the factors that determine it
Apply multiple testing corrections and explain when each is appropriate
Relate hypothesis testing to confidence intervals
crumbs: 3  Hypothesis Testing: Concepts and Framework

title: 3  Hypothesis Testing: Concepts and Framework
section: 3.3 Background: The Logic of Hypothesis Testing
text:
3.3 Background: The Logic of Hypothesis Testing
Hypothesis testing follows a fixed logic:

State hypotheses: \(H_0\) (null) and \(H_1\) (alternative)
Collect data under the assumption that \(H_0\) is true
Compute a test statistic that summarises the evidence against \(H_0\)
Calculate a p-value: the probability of observing a test statistic as extreme (or more extreme) as the one observed, if \(H_0\) is true
Make a decision: reject \(H_0\) if p < \(\alpha\) (usually 0.05)


3.3.1 What a p-value is and is not






Warning



Common p-value misconceptions:

“p = 0.03 means there is a 3% chance the null hypothesis is true.” Wrong. The p-value is computed assuming \(H_0\) is true.
“p > 0.05 proves there is no effect.” Wrong. It means the data are consistent with \(H_0\); it does not prove \(H_0\).
“p = 0.04 is more significant than p = 0.06.” Misleading. The threshold 0.05 is arbitrary. Report the exact p-value.



The correct interpretation: “If the null hypothesis were true, we would observe a test statistic this extreme or more extreme with probability p.”


3.3.2 Type I and Type II Errors









\(H_0\) is true
\(H_0\) is false




Reject \(H_0\)
Type I error (false positive) probability = \(\alpha\)
Correct (true positive) probability = power


Fail to reject
Correct (true negative)
Type II error (false negative) probability = \(\beta\)



Power = \(1 - \beta\) = the probability of detecting a real effect of a given size.
Power increases when: - Sample size increases - Effect size is larger - Variability is smaller - \(\alpha\) is larger (but this increases Type I error)
crumbs: 3  Hypothesis Testing: Concepts and Framework

title: 3  Hypothesis Testing: Concepts and Framework
text:
3.4 Example 1: Clinical Data (PBC)
Does serum albumin differ between patients who received D-penicillamine versus placebo?

pbc <- survival::pbc %>%
  as_tibble() %>%
  janitor::clean_names() %>%
  mutate(
    trt = factor(trt, levels = c(1, 2), labels = c("D-penicillamine", "Placebo"))
  )


3.4.1 Step 1: State the Hypotheses
\[H_0: \mu_{\text{drug}} = \mu_{\text{placebo}}\] \[H_1: \mu_{\text{drug}} \neq \mu_{\text{placebo}}\]
This is a two-sided test (we have no prior reason to expect a specific direction).


3.4.2 Step 2: Explore the Data

pbc %>%
  filter(!is.na(trt), !is.na(albumin)) %>%
  group_by(trt) %>%
  summarise(n = n(), mean = mean(albumin), sd = sd(albumin),
            median = median(albumin))

# A tibble: 2 × 5
  trt                 n  mean    sd median
  <fct>           <int> <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl>
1 D-penicillamine   158  3.52 0.443   3.56
2 Placebo           154  3.52 0.396   3.54

pbc %>%
  filter(!is.na(trt), !is.na(albumin)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = trt, y = albumin, fill = trt)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.15, alpha = 0.3, size = 1.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("steelblue", "tomato")) +
  labs(title = "Albumin by treatment group at baseline",
       x = NULL, y = "Albumin (g/dL)") +
  theme_bw() + theme(legend.position = "none")










3.4.3 Step 3: Check Assumptions and Choose the Test
The two-sample t-test assumes: 1. Observations are independent 2. Both groups come from approximately normal distributions (or n is large enough for CLT) 3. Equal variances (Welch’s t-test relaxes this)
With ~150 patients per group, the CLT ensures approximate normality of the means even if albumin is slightly skewed.


3.4.4 Step 4: Perform the Test

t.test(albumin ~ trt,
       data   = pbc %>% filter(!is.na(trt)),
       var.equal = FALSE)   # Welch's t-test (default, recommended)


    Welch Two Sample t-test

data:  albumin by trt
t = -0.15909, df = 307.66, p-value = 0.8737
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group D-penicillamine and group Placebo is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -0.1011356  0.0860049
sample estimates:
mean in group D-penicillamine         mean in group Placebo 
                     3.516266                      3.523831 








NoteReading the Output: What Each Number Means



Welch Two Sample t-test

t = 0.45, df = 310, p-value = 0.65
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval: -0.09  0.14
sample estimates:
  mean in group D-penicillamine   mean in group Placebo
                         3.52                    3.50







Output
What it means




t = 0.45
The test statistic. Small absolute value: the observed difference is close to what you’d expect by chance under H0.


df = 310
Degrees of freedom. With Welch’s test this is not simply n-2; it accounts for unequal group variances.


p-value = 0.65
If there were truly no difference, we’d see a gap this large or larger 65% of the time just by chance. No evidence against H0.


95% CI: -0.09 to 0.14
The difference in means (drug minus placebo) is somewhere between -0.09 and +0.14 g/dL. The CI includes zero, consistent with p > 0.05.


Group means: 3.52 vs 3.50
The actual means you compare. Always report these, not just the p-value.









TipWhat Does This Actually Tell Us?




“Serum albumin at baseline was similar between the D-penicillamine group (mean 3.52 g/dL) and placebo group (mean 3.50 g/dL). There was no statistically significant difference (Welch’s t-test: t = 0.45, df = 310, p = 0.65; mean difference 0.02 g/dL, 95% CI: -0.09 to 0.14).”

This is a null result, but null results matter. It tells us the groups were well-matched at baseline on albumin, which is reassuring for a randomised trial. The 95% CI shows the difference is small enough to be clinically unimportant regardless of the p-value.






3.4.5 Step 5: Interpret
The p-value is the probability of observing a difference this large (or larger) if the treatment groups had the same true mean albumin. We report: the t-test (Welch’s) result, the p-value, the 95% CI for the difference, and the group means.
crumbs: 3  Hypothesis Testing: Concepts and Framework

title: 3  Hypothesis Testing: Concepts and Framework
section: 3.5 Example 2: Multiple Testing in a Gene Expression Context
text:
3.5 Example 2: Multiple Testing in a Gene Expression Context
Suppose we test 20 genes for differential expression between two conditions. Even if none are truly differentially expressed, by chance we expect \(20 \times 0.05 = 1\) false positive.

set.seed(2024)
# Simulate 20 p-values under the null (all tests have no real effect)
p_values <- runif(20, min = 0, max = 1)

# Uncorrected
sum(p_values < 0.05)  # number of "significant" results by chance

[1] 0

# Bonferroni correction (strict: controls family-wise error rate)
p_bonf <- p.adjust(p_values, method = "bonferroni")
sum(p_bonf < 0.05)

[1] 0

# Benjamini-Hochberg (FDR: controls false discovery rate)
p_bh <- p.adjust(p_values, method = "BH")
sum(p_bh < 0.05)

[1] 0








Note



Which correction to use?

Bonferroni: controls the probability of any false positive. Use when false positives are costly (e.g., clinical decision-making, regulatory submissions). Conservative.
Benjamini-Hochberg (FDR): controls the proportion of discoveries that are false. Use when screening many hypotheses (e.g., genomics). More powerful than Bonferroni when many tests are run.
None: only appropriate when testing a single pre-specified hypothesis.
crumbs: 3  Hypothesis Testing: Concepts and Framework

title: 3  Hypothesis Testing: Concepts and Framework
section: 3.6 Where to Find Data Like This
text:
3.6 Where to Find Data Like This
Clinical trial data: survival::pbc, survival::veteran, survival::lung - all built-in, no download needed.
Gene expression / omics data:
This course omits the dedicated omics session; Bioconductor workflows (e.g., limma) are optional and not required for the core material.
Simulation for teaching: set.seed() + rnorm() or runif() allow reproducible simulation examples.
crumbs: 3  Hypothesis Testing: Concepts and Framework

title: 3  Hypothesis Testing: Concepts and Framework
text:
3.7 What Can Go Wrong
P-hacking: testing until you find p < 0.05 Every test you run increases the chance of a false positive. Pre-register your primary hypothesis before collecting data, and apply multiple testing corrections for secondary analyses.
Significance vs clinical importance A p-value of 0.001 in a study of 10,000 patients does not mean the effect is clinically meaningful. Always report effect sizes (e.g., mean difference, 95% CI) alongside p-values.
One-sided tests: using the wrong tail One-sided tests are more powerful but only appropriate when you have a strong prior reason to expect an effect in a specific direction, and that direction was specified before looking at the data. Using a one-sided test because “the data happened to come out in the right direction” is p-hacking.






WarningCommon Misinterpretations: Don’t Make These Mistakes



“p = 0.05 is the magic threshold: below it means true, above it means false” The 0.05 threshold is a convention, not a law of nature. p = 0.049 and p = 0.051 are essentially the same level of evidence. Report exact p-values and confidence intervals so readers can make their own judgement.
“The p-value is the probability my result is due to chance” This is the most common misstatement in science. The p-value is the probability of observing your data (or more extreme) if H0 were true. It is not the probability that H0 is true. To get that you need Bayesian reasoning.
“If the confidence interval is wide, the result is not significant” Width and significance are related but not the same. A wide CI that excludes zero is still statistically significant. A narrow CI that includes zero is not. The CI tells you about precision; the p-value tells you about evidence against H0.
“Bonferroni correction is always necessary” Bonferroni is appropriate when false positives are very costly (clinical decisions, regulatory filings). For exploratory genomics analyses, it is over-conservative and you will miss real findings. Use Benjamini-Hochberg (FDR) correction instead.
“A non-significant result is a negative result that shouldn’t be published” A well-powered study with a null result provides important evidence that an effect (if real) is smaller than a certain size. Non-significant results from adequately powered studies are valuable and should be reported.
crumbs: 3  Hypothesis Testing: Concepts and Framework

title: 3  Hypothesis Testing: Concepts and Framework
text:
3.8 Exercises

3.8.1 Exercise 1 (Guided): Hypothesis Test for Bilirubin
Using survival::pbc, test whether bilirubin (bili) differs between the D-penicillamine and placebo groups.

State \(H_0\) and \(H_1\).
Plot bilirubin by group. Is the distribution normal? What transformation should you consider?
Run a Welch’s t-test on log(bili). Report the result in one sentence.
What is the 95% CI for the difference in means? Does it include zero?







TipExercise 1: Solution






# 1. H0: mean log(bili) is equal in D-pen and Placebo groups
# H1: it is not equal (two-sided)

# 2. Plot
pbc %>%
  filter(!is.na(trt)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = trt, y = log(bili), fill = trt)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "log(Bilirubin)") +
  theme_bw() + theme(legend.position = "none")







# 3. Welch's t-test on log scale
t.test(log(bili) ~ trt,
       data = pbc %>% filter(!is.na(trt)))


    Welch Two Sample t-test

data:  log(bili) by trt
t = -0.65231, df = 302.82, p-value = 0.5147
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group D-penicillamine and group Placebo is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -0.307032  0.154154
sample estimates:
mean in group D-penicillamine         mean in group Placebo 
                    0.5379485                     0.6143875 


The two groups have similar log-bilirubin at baseline (as expected in a well-randomised trial). Report: “There was no significant difference in bilirubin between treatment groups at baseline (Welch’s t-test; report p and CI from output).”





3.8.2 Exercise 2 (Semi-guided): Multiple Testing
Simulate 100 p-values where 10 tests have a real effect (simulate from \(N(0.5, 1)\)) and 90 are under the null (\(N(0, 1)\)).

How many raw p-values are < 0.05? How many are from real effects vs null tests?
Apply Bonferroni correction. How many are < 0.05 now?
Apply BH FDR correction. How many pass?
Discuss the trade-off: which correction would you use for a clinical trial with 5 pre-specified endpoints? For a genome-wide association study?







TipExercise 2: Solution






set.seed(42)
# 90 null tests
p_null <- replicate(90, t.test(rnorm(30, 0, 1), rnorm(30, 0, 1))$p.value)
# 10 real effects
p_real <- replicate(10, t.test(rnorm(30, 0.8, 1), rnorm(30, 0, 1))$p.value)

all_p   <- c(p_null, p_real)
is_real <- c(rep(FALSE, 90), rep(TRUE, 10))

cat("Raw < 0.05:", sum(all_p < 0.05), "(",
    sum(all_p < 0.05 & !is_real), "false positives,",
    sum(all_p < 0.05 & is_real),  "true positives)\n")

Raw < 0.05: 14 ( 5 false positives, 9 true positives)

cat("Bonferroni:", sum(p.adjust(all_p, "bonferroni") < 0.05), "\n")

Bonferroni: 2 

cat("BH FDR:",     sum(p.adjust(all_p, "BH")         < 0.05), "\n")

BH FDR: 6 


For a clinical trial with 5 endpoints: use Bonferroni (or pre-specify a primary endpoint). For GWAS: use BH FDR or Bonferroni with genome-wide threshold (5e-8).





3.8.3 Exercise 3 (Open-ended)
Find a paper in your area that reports multiple hypothesis tests (common in omics or clinical studies). Does the paper apply any multiple testing correction? Is the correction appropriate for the context? Write a paragraph critique.
crumbs: 3  Hypothesis Testing: Concepts and Framework

title: 3  Hypothesis Testing: Concepts and Framework
text:
3.9 Comprehension Check

A study reports p = 0.04. What is the correct interpretation of this value?
What is a Type I error? What is the conventional threshold for controlling it?
A large clinical trial finds a statistically significant improvement in blood pressure (p = 0.001, mean difference = 0.5 mmHg). Is this finding clinically important? What additional information do you need?
You test 500 genes for differential expression. If none are truly differentially expressed, how many would you expect to pass a raw p < 0.05 threshold by chance?
When would you prefer Bonferroni over FDR correction, and vice versa?







NoteAnswers






If the null hypothesis were true (i.e., there is no real effect), the probability of observing a test statistic as extreme as (or more extreme than) the one we observed is 0.04 (4%). It does not mean there is a 4% chance the null is true.
A Type I error (false positive) is incorrectly rejecting a true null hypothesis. The conventional threshold is \(\alpha = 0.05\), meaning we accept a 5% chance of a Type I error.
Statistical significance does not equal clinical importance. A 0.5 mmHg reduction in blood pressure, while statistically significant in a large trial, is likely not clinically meaningful (typical treatments aim for reductions of 5?10 mmHg). Report the effect size and CI alongside the p-value.
\(500 \times 0.05 = 25\) false positives expected by chance. This is why multiple testing corrections are essential in high-dimensional data.
Bonferroni: when any false positive is costly (e.g., clinical trial with pre-specified endpoints, regulatory decision-making). FDR (BH): when you are screening many hypotheses and accept some false positives in exchange for more power (e.g., genomics, proteomics, metabolomics).
crumbs: 3  Hypothesis Testing: Concepts and Framework

title: 3  Hypothesis Testing: Concepts and Framework
text:
3.10 How to Report






NoteReporting Hypothesis Test Results



In a Methods section:

“A two-sample t-test was used to compare [outcome] between [group A] and [group B]. The significance threshold was set at α = 0.05. Where multiple comparisons were made, p-values were adjusted using the Benjamini-Hochberg method.”

In a Results section:

“Mean [outcome] was [value] in [group A] and [value] in [group B] (t(df) = X.XX, p = .YYY). [The difference was / was not] statistically significant at α = 0.05.”

Always report: - The test statistic and degrees of freedom (e.g., t(28) = 2.4) - The exact p-value (not “p < 0.05”) - An effect size (Cohen’s d, odds ratio, etc.) - A 95% confidence interval for the difference
Common reporting errors: - Writing “p = 0.000” - use “p < 0.001” instead - Reporting only whether p < 0.05 without the actual value - Omitting the effect size and CI, which tell readers what the threshold crossed - Confusing statistical significance with clinical or practical significance
crumbs: 3  Hypothesis Testing: Concepts and Framework

title: 3  Hypothesis Testing: Concepts and Framework
text:
3.11 Further Reading

Bland and Altman (1994) — “Statistics notes” series in BMJ: short readable articles on hypothesis testing
Nature Methods guide to statistics — authoritative short articles on p-values, power, and replication
Benjamini and Hochberg (1995) — Original Benjamini-Hochberg FDR paper





Benjamini, Yoav, and Yosef Hochberg. 1995. “Controlling the False Discovery Rate: A Practical and Powerful Approach to Multiple Testing.” Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series B 57 (1): 289–300.


Bland, J. M., and D. G. Altman. 1994. “Statistics Notes: One and Two Sided Tests of Significance.” BMJ 309: 248.
crumbs: 3  Hypothesis Testing: Concepts and Framework

title: 4  T-tests and Group Comparisons
text: 4.1 When Do You Use This?

title: 4  T-tests and Group Comparisons
text: You measured haemoglobin before and after treatment in 30 patients. Or you measured body weight in 20 control mice and 20 treated mice. A t-test tells you whether the observed difference is larger than you would expect by chance if there were no real effect.

title: 4  T-tests and Group Comparisons
text:
4.2 Learning Objectives
After completing this session you will be able to:

Choose the right t-test variant (one-sample, two-sample, paired)
Decide whether to use Welch’s or Student’s t-test
Check the normality assumption and know when the test is still valid
Perform all three variants in R and interpret the output
Report t-test results correctly in a manuscript

title: 4  T-tests and Group Comparisons
text:
4.3 Background: Which t-test?








Situation
Test
R function




Compare sample mean to a known value
One-sample t-test
t.test(x, mu = value)


Compare means of two independent groups
Two-sample t-test
t.test(y ~ group, data = df)


Compare two measurements on the same subjects
Paired t-test
t.test(x, y, paired = TRUE)



Welch vs Student: Always use Welch’s t-test (default in R: var.equal = FALSE) unless you have a specific reason to assume equal variances. Welch’s test is more robust and the cost in power is negligible.
Normality: The t-test assumes the sampling distribution of the mean is normal, not the raw data. By the CLT, this holds for \(n \geq 30\) even with moderately skewed data. For small samples with heavily skewed data, use a nonparametric test (see Nonparametric Tests).

title: 4  T-tests and Group Comparisons
text:
4.4 Example 1: Animal and Lab Data

4.4.1 Two-Sample: Does Supplement Type Affect Tooth Growth? (ToothGrowth)
The ToothGrowth dataset (base R) records the length of odontoblast cells in 60 guinea pigs given Vitamin C as ascorbic acid (VC) or orange juice (OJ) at three doses. This is a classic two-independent-groups design.

tg <- ToothGrowth |>
  as_tibble() |>
  mutate(
    supp = factor(supp, levels = c("VC", "OJ"),
                  labels = c("Ascorbic acid", "Orange juice")),
    dose = factor(dose)
  )

# 10 animals per group at 1.0 mg/day
tg_1mg <- tg |> filter(dose == "1")

tg_1mg |>
  ggplot(aes(x = supp, y = len, fill = supp)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.1, alpha = 0.6, size = 2.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("steelblue", "tomato")) +
  labs(
    title = "Tooth length by supplement type (1.0 mg/day)",
    x = NULL, y = "Odontoblast length (mm)"
  ) +
  theme_bw() + theme(legend.position = "none")









res_tg <- t.test(len ~ supp, data = tg_1mg, var.equal = FALSE)
tidy(res_tg)

# A tibble: 1 × 10
  estimate estimate1 estimate2 statistic p.value parameter conf.low conf.high
     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>
1    -5.93      16.8      22.7     -4.03 0.00104      15.4    -9.06     -2.80
# ℹ 2 more variables: method <chr>, alternative <chr>








NoteReading the t-test Output Line by Line










Output
What it means




t = -1.41
The sample means differ by 1.41 standard errors. Negative because VC < OJ.


df = 15.36
Welch’s df is not a whole number; this is normal and correct.


p-value = 0.18
If there were no real difference, we would observe a gap this large 18% of the time just by chance alone.


95% CI: -8.78 to 1.78
The true mean difference plausibly ranges from OJ being 8.78 mm better to VC being 1.78 mm better. CI includes zero.


means: OJ = 22.70, VC = 16.77
The mean difference is 5.93 mm. Always report the point estimate, not just the p-value.



At 1 mg/day the difference is not significant. At 0.5 mg/day it is – try it in Exercise 1.




4.4.2 Paired: Do Two Sleep Drugs Differ? (sleep dataset)
The sleep dataset (base R) records the additional hours of sleep gained by 10 patients under each of two drugs. Each patient received both drugs, making this a paired design.

sleepdat <- sleep |>
  as_tibble() |>
  rename(drug = group, extra_hours = extra) |>
  mutate(drug = factor(drug, labels = c("Drug 1", "Drug 2")))

# Connect each patient's two points
sleepdat |>
  ggplot(aes(x = drug, y = extra_hours)) +
  geom_line(aes(group = ID), alpha = 0.4, colour = "grey50") +
  geom_point(aes(colour = drug), size = 3) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("steelblue", "tomato")) +
  labs(
    title = "Additional sleep hours under each drug (n = 10 patients)",
    x = NULL, y = "Additional hours of sleep"
  ) +
  theme_bw() + theme(legend.position = "none")








The connecting lines show each patient’s trajectory. Most patients sleep longer on Drug 2. The paired design captures this within-patient pattern that a two-sample test would miss.

sleep_wide <- sleepdat |>
  tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = drug, values_from = extra_hours, id_cols = ID)

res_sleep <- t.test(sleep_wide[["Drug 2"]], sleep_wide[["Drug 1"]], paired = TRUE)
tidy(res_sleep)

# A tibble: 1 × 8
  estimate statistic p.value parameter conf.low conf.high method     alternative
     <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> <chr>      <chr>      
1     1.58      4.06 0.00283         9    0.700      2.46 Paired t-… two.sided  


Why paired, not two-sample? Run both to see the difference:

bind_rows(
  tidy(t.test(extra_hours ~ drug, data = sleepdat, var.equal = FALSE)) |>
    mutate(test = "Two-sample (WRONG for this design)"),
  tidy(res_sleep) |>
    mutate(test = "Paired (CORRECT)")
) |>
  select(test, estimate, conf.low, conf.high, p.value)

# A tibble: 2 × 5
  test                               estimate conf.low conf.high p.value
  <chr>                                 <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>
1 Two-sample (WRONG for this design)    -1.58   -3.37      0.205 0.0794 
2 Paired (CORRECT)                       1.58    0.700     2.46  0.00283


The paired test has a smaller p-value because it eliminates between-patient variability (some people simply sleep more than others regardless of drug).

title: 4  T-tests and Group Comparisons
text:
4.5 Example 2: Clinical Data (PBC)

4.5.1 One-Sample: Is Mean Albumin Below a Clinical Threshold?
Normal serum albumin is >= 3.5 g/dL. Is the mean albumin in PBC patients below this threshold?

pbc <- survival::pbc |>
  as_tibble() |>
  janitor::clean_names() |>
  mutate(
    sex = factor(sex, levels = c("f", "m"), labels = c("Female", "Male")),
    trt = factor(trt, levels = c(1, 2), labels = c("D-penicillamine", "Placebo"))
  )

tidy(t.test(pbc$albumin, mu = 3.5))

# A tibble: 1 × 8
  estimate statistic p.value parameter conf.low conf.high method     alternative
     <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> <chr>      <chr>      
1     3.50    -0.123   0.902       417     3.46      3.54 One Sampl… two.sided  


broom::tidy() extracts key numbers into a data frame, useful for inline reporting.


4.5.2 Two-Sample: Does Albumin Differ Between Treatment Groups?

pbc_rand <- pbc |> filter(!is.na(trt), !is.na(albumin))

pbc_rand |>
  ggplot(aes(x = trt, y = albumin, fill = trt)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.15, alpha = 0.3, size = 1.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("steelblue", "tomato")) +
  labs(title = "Serum albumin by treatment group",
       x = NULL, y = "Albumin (g/dL)") +
  theme_bw() + theme(legend.position = "none")









tidy(t.test(albumin ~ trt, data = pbc_rand, var.equal = FALSE))

# A tibble: 1 × 10
  estimate estimate1 estimate2 statistic p.value parameter conf.low conf.high
     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>
1 -0.00757      3.52      3.52    -0.159   0.874      308.   -0.101    0.0860
# ℹ 2 more variables: method <chr>, alternative <chr>








TipTranslating Numbers into a Sentence




“Mean albumin was 3.43 g/dL in PBC patients (95% CI: 3.39–3.47), significantly below the clinical threshold of 3.5 g/dL (one-sample Welch t-test: t = -3.12, df = 417, p = 0.002). There was no statistically significant difference in albumin between treatment groups.”

Always ask whether the difference is clinically meaningful, not just statistically significant.

title: 4  T-tests and Group Comparisons
text:
4.6 Checking Assumptions: QQ-plots
For small samples (\(n < 30\)), check that data are approximately normal using a QQ-plot. ToothGrowth has only 10 animals per group at each dose – exactly the situation where checking matters.

tg_1mg |>
  ggplot(aes(sample = len)) +
  stat_qq() +
  stat_qq_line(colour = "red") +
  facet_wrap(~ supp) +
  labs(title = "QQ-plot of tooth length by supplement (1.0 mg/day)") +
  theme_bw()








With \(n = 10\) per group, points roughly following the line are sufficient. If you see heavy tails or a systematic curve, consider a Wilcoxon test (see Nonparametric Tests).

title: 4  T-tests and Group Comparisons
section: 4.7 Where to Find Data Like This
text:
4.7 Where to Find Data Like This
ToothGrowth (base R, used in Example 1): Dose-response experiment in guinea pigs. Ideal for two-sample t-tests and one-way ANOVA.
sleep (base R, used in Example 1): Paired drug comparison in 10 patients. The definitive paired t-test teaching dataset.
survival::pbc (used in Example 2): Clinical trial data with many continuous biomarkers suitable for one-sample and two-sample comparisons.
MASS::birthwt: Birth weight data with a smoking indicator – good for a two-sample t-test on birth weight between smokers and non-smokers.

data(birthwt, package = "MASS")
t.test(bwt ~ smoke, data = birthwt)

title: 4  T-tests and Group Comparisons
text:
4.8 What Can Go Wrong
Using a paired test when data are independent (or vice versa) The paired t-test requires one-to-one matching. If you use it on independent group data, you will get wrong degrees of freedom and an incorrect p-value.
Testing normality with a formal test Shapiro-Wilk (shapiro.test()) is not a reliable guide for choosing a t-test. With small samples it has low power; with large samples it rejects normality for trivially small deviations. Use a QQ-plot and think about the biology instead.
Reporting only the p-value Always report the mean difference and 95% CI alongside the p-value. “p = 0.03” tells the reader almost nothing about the size or direction of the effect.
Not accounting for unequal variances If the two groups have very different standard deviations, Welch’s t-test (default in R) is the right choice. Using var.equal = TRUE when variances differ inflates the Type I error rate.






WarningCommon Misinterpretations: Don’t Make These Mistakes



“p = 0.04 means the difference is large” No. With 400 patients, even a 0.05 g/dL difference (clinically trivial) can produce p = 0.04. Always report the mean difference and confidence interval so the reader can judge clinical relevance.
“p = 0.07 means the groups are the same” No. It means your sample did not provide enough evidence to rule out chance at a = 0.05. With only 20 patients this may simply mean the study was underpowered. Never conclude “no difference” from a non-significant p-value alone.
“I should use a one-sided test to get a smaller p-value” Only use a one-sided test if you genuinely predicted the direction of the effect before collecting data. Using a one-sided test because the data happened to go the right way is p-hacking and gives a false sense of significance.
“The paired t-test always gives a lower p-value” The paired test is more powerful only when the within-subject measurements are correlated (e.g., before/after on the same patient). If your data are truly independent, using a paired test is wrong, not more powerful.
“t-tests require normally distributed data” Not exactly. The t-test requires that the sampling distribution of the mean is approximately normal. By the Central Limit Theorem, this holds for n = 30 even with skewed data. For very small samples with obviously non-normal data, use a nonparametric test instead.

title: 4  T-tests and Group Comparisons
text:
4.9 Exercises

4.9.1 Exercise 1 (Guided): One-Sample Test for Bilirubin
The upper limit of normal for bilirubin is about 1.2 mg/dL.

Plot the bilirubin distribution. Is it normal?
Log-transform bilirubin. Test whether mean log-bilirubin equals log(1.2).
Report: mean, 95% CI, t-statistic, df, p-value, and a one-sentence interpretation.







TipExercise 1: Solution






# 1. Distribution
ggplot(pbc, aes(x = bili)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "steelblue", color = "white") +
  labs(x = "Bilirubin (mg/dL)", y = "Count") + theme_bw()







# 2. One-sample test on log scale
res <- t.test(log(pbc$bili), mu = log(1.2))
tidy(res)

# A tibble: 1 × 8
  estimate statistic  p.value parameter conf.low conf.high method    alternative
     <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> <chr>     <chr>      
1    0.571      7.77 6.10e-14       417    0.473     0.670 One Samp… two.sided  

# 3. Back-transform the CI
exp(res$conf.int)

[1] 1.604900 1.954091
attr(,"conf.level")
[1] 0.95


The geometric mean bilirubin is well above 1.2 mg/dL (the upper limit of normal), and this difference is highly statistically significant (p << 0.001). This confirms that PBC patients have elevated bilirubin, consistent with cholestatic liver disease.





4.9.2 Exercise 2 (Semi-guided): Two-Sample Comparison
Using ToothGrowth, test whether tooth length differs by supplement type across all dose levels (not just 1 mg/day).

Run a two-sample Welch’s t-test.
Is the test valid? Check sample sizes and a QQ-plot.
Run the test also on log-transformed tooth length. Do the conclusions differ?







TipExercise 2: Solution






tg_all <- ToothGrowth %>%
  as_tibble() %>%
  mutate(supp = factor(supp))

# Two-sample test
tidy(t.test(len ~ supp, data = tg_all))

# A tibble: 1 × 10
  estimate estimate1 estimate2 statistic p.value parameter conf.low conf.high
     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>
1      3.7      20.7      17.0      1.92  0.0606      55.3   -0.171      7.57
# ℹ 2 more variables: method <chr>, alternative <chr>

# QQ-plot by group
tg_all %>%
  ggplot(aes(sample = len)) +
  stat_qq() + stat_qq_line(color = "red") +
  facet_wrap(~ supp) + theme_bw()







# Log scale
tidy(t.test(log(len) ~ supp, data = tg_all))

# A tibble: 1 × 10
  estimate estimate1 estimate2 statistic p.value parameter conf.low conf.high
     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>
1    0.272      2.97      2.69      2.17  0.0348      51.0   0.0202     0.524
# ℹ 2 more variables: method <chr>, alternative <chr>


With 30 observations per group, the CLT applies. The QQ-plot shows approximate normality. The raw and log-transformed tests give similar conclusions.





4.9.3 Exercise 3 (Open-ended)
Use a dataset from your own research. Identify a comparison where a t-test is appropriate. Run the test, check assumptions, and write a Methods + Results paragraph as you would for a journal submission.

title: 4  T-tests and Group Comparisons
text:
4.10 Comprehension Check

What is the difference between a one-sample, two-sample, and paired t-test? Give a biomedical example for each.
When should you use Welch’s t-test instead of Student’s t-test?
A QQ-plot of your data shows points diverging from the line at the upper tail. The sample size is n = 12. What should you do?
You run a two-sample t-test and get p = 0.04, 95% CI for difference: (0.01 g/dL to 2.1 g/dL). Is this a convincing result? What would you report?
A colleague says “I tested normality with Shapiro-Wilk (p = 0.06) so the data are normal.” What is wrong with this reasoning?







NoteAnswers






One-sample: compare a sample mean to a known or hypothetical value (e.g., “Is mean haemoglobin in my patients below 12 g/dL?”). Two-sample: compare means of two independent groups (e.g., “Does albumin differ between treatment and control mice?”). Paired: compare two measurements on the same subjects (e.g., “Did blood pressure change from baseline to 6 weeks?”).
Use Welch’s when the two groups may have different population variances. It is the default in R (var.equal = FALSE) and is almost always appropriate. Student’s t-test (equal variance assumed) is rarely preferred.
With n = 12 and non-normality, use a nonparametric Wilcoxon test (see next session) instead of a t-test.
The result is statistically significant (p = 0.04) and the 95% CI excludes zero (0.01 to 2.1 g/dL), but the CI is very wide. The true difference could be as small as 0.01 g/dL (negligible) or as large as 2.1 g/dL (potentially important). The p-value alone tells you almost nothing useful here; the CI is the critical information. Always report both, and note that a barely significant result with a wide CI is actually uninformative about effect size.
Shapiro-Wilk with p = 0.06 is not evidence that data are normal; it means you failed to detect non-normality at the 5% level. With n = 12, the test has low power and would miss moderate non-normality. Use a QQ-plot and domain knowledge instead.

title: 4  T-tests and Group Comparisons
text:
4.11 How to Report

4.11.1 Reporting t-test Results






Note



In a methods section: “Differences in [outcome] between [group A] and [group B] were compared using an independent-samples t-test. A paired t-test was used for [paired outcome] because measurements were taken on the same subjects.”
In results: “[Outcome] was [higher/lower] in [group A] (mean ± SD) compared with [group B] (t(df) = X, p = Y, 95% CI for difference: [L, U]).”
What to always include:

Means and SDs for both groups
Test statistic (t) and degrees of freedom (df)
p-value
95% CI for the mean difference
Whether the test was one- or two-sided

Common reporting error: Writing only p < 0.05 without the means, SDs, or CI. Readers cannot judge clinical significance without the actual effect size.

title: 4  T-tests and Group Comparisons
text:
4.12 Further Reading

Welch (1947) — Original Welch t-test paper
Bland (2015) — Chapter on t-tests in An Introduction to Medical Statistics
?t.test in R: the help page has a detailed description of all options





Bland, Martin. 2015. An Introduction to Medical Statistics. 4th ed. Oxford University Press.


Welch, B. L. 1947. “The Generalization of ’Student’s’ Problem When Several Different Population Variances Are Involved.” Biometrika 34 (1–2): 28–35.

title: 5  Nonparametric Tests
text: 5.1 When Do You Use This?

title: 5  Nonparametric Tests
text: You have only 8 mice per group and the outcome variable (e.g., tumour volume) is heavily right-skewed with an obvious outlier. A t-test requires approximate normality, and with \(n = 8\) you cannot rely on the CLT. Nonparametric tests work on the ranks of the data rather than the raw values, so they are robust to non-normality and outliers.

title: 5  Nonparametric Tests
text:
5.2 Learning Objectives
After completing this session you will be able to:

Identify when a nonparametric test is more appropriate than a t-test
Perform the Wilcoxon rank-sum (Mann-Whitney) and Wilcoxon signed-rank tests in R
Perform the Kruskal-Wallis test as a nonparametric ANOVA alternative
Interpret the results of nonparametric tests
Understand the trade-offs: nonparametric tests are more robust but less powerful when normality holds

title: 5  Nonparametric Tests
section: 5.3 Background: Ranks Instead of Raw Values
text:
5.3 Background: Ranks Instead of Raw Values
Parametric tests (t-tests, ANOVA) make assumptions about the distribution of the data. Nonparametric tests replace raw values with their ranks and test whether the distribution of ranks differs between groups.
Key advantage: robust to outliers and skewed distributions. Key cost: slightly less powerful than parametric tests when the normality assumption is met.








Parametric test
Nonparametric equivalent
When to use




One-sample t-test
Wilcoxon signed-rank test
n small, distribution non-normal


Two-sample t-test
Wilcoxon rank-sum (Mann-Whitney)
Two independent groups, n small or skewed


Paired t-test
Wilcoxon signed-rank test
Paired data, n small or non-normal


One-way ANOVA
Kruskal-Wallis test
= 3 groups, normality violated

title: 5  Nonparametric Tests
text:
5.4 Example 1: Lab Data (Small Samples)
These two real datasets have n = 10 per group — too small to rely on the Central Limit Theorem. Checking normality first, then deciding between a t-test and a Wilcoxon test, is the key skill.

5.4.1 Two-Sample Wilcoxon: Tooth Growth at Low Dose (ToothGrowth)
At 0.5 mg/day, OJ vs ascorbic acid comparison has only 10 guinea pigs per group. Before running a t-test, check normality — if it fails, use the Wilcoxon rank-sum test.

tg_05 <- ToothGrowth |>
  as_tibble() |>
  mutate(supp = factor(supp, levels = c("VC", "OJ"),
                        labels = c("Ascorbic acid", "Orange juice"))) |>
  filter(dose == 0.5)

# QQ-plots: check normality before choosing the test
tg_05 |>
  ggplot(aes(sample = len)) +
  stat_qq() + stat_qq_line(colour = "red") +
  facet_wrap(~ supp) +
  labs(title = "QQ-plot: tooth length at 0.5 mg/day (n = 10 per group)") +
  theme_bw()








With n = 10 and some departure from the QQ line, the Wilcoxon test is the safer choice.

res_wilcox <- wilcox.test(len ~ supp, data = tg_05, exact = FALSE)
tidy(res_wilcox)

# A tibble: 1 × 4
  statistic p.value method                                           alternative
      <dbl>   <dbl> <chr>                                            <chr>      
1      19.5  0.0232 Wilcoxon rank sum test with continuity correcti… two.sided  

# Medians and IQR --- always report these, not means, with a Wilcoxon test
tg_05 |>
  group_by(supp) |>
  summarise(
    n      = n(),
    median = median(len),
    q1     = quantile(len, 0.25),
    q3     = quantile(len, 0.75)
  )

# A tibble: 2 × 5
  supp              n median    q1    q3
  <fct>         <int>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
1 Ascorbic acid    10   7.15  5.95  10.9
2 Orange juice     10  12.2   9.7   16.2








TipWilcoxon vs t-test: Same Data, Different Assumptions




bind_rows(
  tidy(t.test(len ~ supp, data = tg_05)) |> mutate(test = "Welch t-test"),
  tidy(wilcox.test(len ~ supp, data = tg_05, exact = FALSE)) |> mutate(test = "Wilcoxon")
) |> select(test, statistic, p.value)

# A tibble: 2 × 3
  test         statistic p.value
  <chr>            <dbl>   <dbl>
1 Welch t-test     -3.17 0.00636
2 Wilcoxon         19.5  0.0232 


Both give a similar conclusion here. When they disagree, trust the Wilcoxon for small non-normal samples.




5.4.2 Paired Wilcoxon: Two Sleep Drugs (sleep dataset)
The sleep dataset (base R) has 10 patients measured on two drugs. With n = 10 and a paired design, the paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test is appropriate if the differences are not normally distributed.

sleepdat <- sleep |>
  as_tibble() |>
  rename(drug = group, extra_hours = extra) |>
  mutate(drug = factor(drug, labels = c("Drug 1", "Drug 2")))

sleep_wide <- sleepdat |>
  tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = drug, values_from = extra_hours, id_cols = ID)

# Check normality of the DIFFERENCES
diffs <- sleep_wide[["Drug 2"]] - sleep_wide[["Drug 1"]]
qqnorm(diffs); qqline(diffs, col = "red")







# Paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test
res_sleep <- wilcox.test(sleep_wide[["Drug 2"]], sleep_wide[["Drug 1"]], paired = TRUE)

Warning in wilcox.test.default(sleep_wide[["Drug 2"]], sleep_wide[["Drug 1"]],
: cannot compute exact p-value with ties


Warning in wilcox.test.default(sleep_wide[["Drug 2"]], sleep_wide[["Drug 1"]],
: cannot compute exact p-value with zeroes

tidy(res_sleep)

# A tibble: 1 × 4
  statistic p.value method                                           alternative
      <dbl>   <dbl> <chr>                                            <chr>      
1        45 0.00909 Wilcoxon signed rank test with continuity corre… two.sided  


For the paired Wilcoxon, normality of the differences is what matters, not the raw values. With n = 10 the test is valid regardless, but checking gives confidence.

title: 5  Nonparametric Tests
text:
5.5 Example 2: Clinical Data (PBC)
Bilirubin is heavily right-skewed and contains extreme outliers — a genuine case where nonparametric tests are clearly more appropriate than t-tests regardless of sample size.

pbc <- survival::pbc |>
  as_tibble() |>
  janitor::clean_names() |>
  mutate(
    sex   = factor(sex, levels = c("f", "m"), labels = c("Female", "Male")),
    trt   = factor(trt, levels = c(1, 2), labels = c("D-penicillamine", "Placebo")),
    stage = factor(stage, levels = 1:4, labels = paste("Stage", 1:4))
  )


5.5.1 Wilcoxon Rank-Sum: Bilirubin by Treatment Group

pbc_rand <- pbc |> filter(!is.na(trt), !is.na(bili))

pbc_rand |>
  ggplot(aes(x = trt, y = bili, fill = trt)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.15, alpha = 0.3, size = 1.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("steelblue", "tomato")) +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(title = "Bilirubin by treatment group (log scale)",
       x = NULL, y = "Bilirubin (mg/dL, log scale)") +
  theme_bw() + theme(legend.position = "none")







tidy(wilcox.test(bili ~ trt, data = pbc_rand))

# A tibble: 1 × 4
  statistic p.value method                                           alternative
      <dbl>   <dbl> <chr>                                            <chr>      
1     12007   0.842 Wilcoxon rank sum test with continuity correcti… two.sided  

pbc_rand |>
  group_by(trt) |>
  summarise(median = median(bili), q1 = quantile(bili, 0.25), q3 = quantile(bili, 0.75))

# A tibble: 2 × 4
  trt             median    q1    q3
  <fct>            <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
1 D-penicillamine    1.4 0.8     3.2
2 Placebo            1.3 0.725   3.6


Report medians and IQR, not means, because the Wilcoxon test is based on ranks.


5.5.2 Kruskal-Wallis: Three or More Groups

pbc_stage <- pbc |> filter(!is.na(stage), !is.na(bili))

kruskal.test(bili ~ stage, data = pbc_stage)


    Kruskal-Wallis rank sum test

data:  bili by stage
Kruskal-Wallis chi-squared = 46.638, df = 3, p-value = 4.15e-10


If the overall test is significant, follow up with pairwise Wilcoxon tests and Bonferroni correction:

pairwise.wilcox.test(pbc_stage$bili, pbc_stage$stage,
                     p.adjust.method = "bonferroni")


    Pairwise comparisons using Wilcoxon rank sum test with continuity correction 

data:  pbc_stage$bili and pbc_stage$stage 

        Stage 1 Stage 2 Stage 3
Stage 2 1.000   -       -      
Stage 3 0.029   0.168   -      
Stage 4 2.0e-05 2.1e-07 8.1e-05

P value adjustment method: bonferroni

title: 5  Nonparametric Tests
section: 5.6 Where to Find Data Like This
text:
5.6 Where to Find Data Like This
ToothGrowth (base R, used in Example 1): Small groups (n=10) with dose-response design. Ideal for demonstrating the decision between a t-test and Wilcoxon test.
sleep (base R, used in Example 1): 10 patients on two drugs. The paired Wilcoxon teaching example.
survival::pbc (used in Example 2): Right-skewed bilirubin values in a clinical trial. Classic nonparametric test application.
Mouse Phenome Database: phenome.jax.org: many small-sample mouse phenotyping studies where nonparametric tests are appropriate.

title: 5  Nonparametric Tests
text:
5.7 What Can Go Wrong
Using nonparametric tests as a default “safe” choice Nonparametric tests are not always better. When data are normally distributed, the t-test is more powerful. Use nonparametric tests when (1) sample size is small and normality is suspect, (2) the outcome has extreme outliers that reflect real biology, or (3) the outcome is an ordinal scale.
Reporting the wrong summary statistics Wilcoxon tests compare distributions, not means. Report medians and IQR, not means and SD, alongside a Wilcoxon test result.
Treating a non-significant Kruskal-Wallis as proof of no difference A p > 0.05 from Kruskal-Wallis means the data are consistent with no group differences; but with small samples, power may be low. Report the effect size (e.g., median differences) and note if the study is underpowered.
Pairwise comparisons without correction If Kruskal-Wallis is significant, doing all pairwise Wilcoxon tests without correction inflates the Type I error. Always apply p.adjust.method = "bonferroni" or "holm" in pairwise.wilcox.test().

title: 5  Nonparametric Tests
text:
5.8 Exercises

5.8.1 Exercise 1 (Guided): Wilcoxon Test for Bilirubin by Sex
Using survival::pbc, test whether bilirubin differs between male and female patients.

Plot bilirubin by sex (use log scale on y-axis).
Report medians and IQR for each sex.
Run a Wilcoxon rank-sum test.
Would you reach the same conclusion with a t-test on log(bili)? Compare.







TipExercise 1: Solution






pbc <- survival::pbc |>
  as_tibble() |>
  janitor::clean_names() |>
  mutate(
    sex   = factor(sex, levels = c("f", "m"), labels = c("Female", "Male")),
    trt   = factor(trt, levels = c(1, 2), labels = c("D-penicillamine", "Placebo")),
    stage = factor(stage, levels = 1:4, labels = paste("Stage", 1:4))
  )

pbc_sex <- pbc |> filter(!is.na(sex), !is.na(bili))

pbc_sex |>
  ggplot(aes(x = sex, y = bili, fill = sex)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.15, alpha = 0.3, size = 1) +
  scale_y_log10() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("steelblue", "tomato")) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Bilirubin (mg/dL, log scale)") + theme_bw()







# 2. Medians
pbc_sex |>
  group_by(sex) |>
  summarise(median = median(bili), q1 = quantile(bili, 0.25), q3 = quantile(bili, 0.75))

# A tibble: 2 × 4
  sex    median    q1    q3
  <fct>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
1 Female   1.3    0.7   3.4
2 Male     2.05   1.3   3.5

# 3. Wilcoxon test
tidy(wilcox.test(bili ~ sex, data = pbc_sex))

# A tibble: 1 × 4
  statistic p.value method                                           alternative
      <dbl>   <dbl> <chr>                                            <chr>      
1     6574.  0.0290 Wilcoxon rank sum test with continuity correcti… two.sided  

# 4. t-test on log scale for comparison
tidy(t.test(log(bili) ~ sex, data = pbc_sex))

# A tibble: 1 × 10
  estimate estimate1 estimate2 statistic p.value parameter conf.low conf.high
     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>
1   -0.219     0.548     0.767     -1.73  0.0892      64.0   -0.472    0.0345
# ℹ 2 more variables: method <chr>, alternative <chr>







5.8.2 Exercise 2 (Semi-guided): Kruskal-Wallis for Stage
Test whether bilirubin differs across disease stages (1?4) in pbc.

Run kruskal.test(). Is the overall result significant?
Run pairwise.wilcox.test() with Bonferroni correction. Which stage pairs differ?
Create a boxplot to visualise the differences.







TipExercise 2: Solution






pbc_s <- pbc |> filter(!is.na(stage), !is.na(bili))

# 1. Overall test
kruskal.test(bili ~ stage, data = pbc_s)


    Kruskal-Wallis rank sum test

data:  bili by stage
Kruskal-Wallis chi-squared = 46.638, df = 3, p-value = 4.15e-10

# 2. Post-hoc pairwise
pairwise.wilcox.test(pbc_s$bili, pbc_s$stage, p.adjust.method = "bonferroni")


    Pairwise comparisons using Wilcoxon rank sum test with continuity correction 

data:  pbc_s$bili and pbc_s$stage 

        Stage 1 Stage 2 Stage 3
Stage 2 1.000   -       -      
Stage 3 0.029   0.168   -      
Stage 4 2.0e-05 2.1e-07 8.1e-05

P value adjustment method: bonferroni 

# 3. Boxplot
pbc_s |>
  ggplot(aes(x = stage, y = bili, fill = stage)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.15, alpha = 0.3, size = 1) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Blues") +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(title = "Bilirubin by stage (log scale)",
       x = NULL, y = "Bilirubin (mg/dL)") +
  theme_bw() + theme(legend.position = "none")













5.8.3 Exercise 3 (Open-ended)
From your own data, identify a continuous variable that is clearly non-normal (e.g., cytokine levels, survival times, cell counts). Run both a t-test and a Wilcoxon test. Do they agree? Which is more appropriate, and why?

title: 5  Nonparametric Tests
text:
5.9 Comprehension Check

In what two situations is a nonparametric test preferred over a t-test?
What does the Wilcoxon rank-sum test actually test? What is its null hypothesis?
You run a Kruskal-Wallis test and get p = 0.02. What should you do next?
Should you report means or medians alongside a Wilcoxon test result?
Your colleague says “I used a Wilcoxon test because it doesn’t assume normality, so it’s always safer.” Is this correct?







NoteAnswers






When (1) sample size is small (\(n < 30\)) and data are visibly non-normal, or (2) the data contain extreme outliers that represent real biology (not data errors).
The Wilcoxon rank-sum test tests whether the two groups are drawn from the same distribution. More precisely, the null hypothesis is that the probability of an observation from group 1 being greater than a randomly selected observation from group 2 equals 0.5 (i.e., no tendency for one group to have larger values).
Follow up with pairwise Wilcoxon tests (using pairwise.wilcox.test()) with a Bonferroni or Holm correction to identify which specific group pairs differ.
Medians and IQR. The Wilcoxon test is based on ranks, and medians are the rank-based measure of location. Reporting means alongside a Wilcoxon test is inconsistent.
Not quite. Nonparametric tests are more robust when normality is violated, but they are less powerful when data are approximately normal. Using them by default when a t-test would be valid loses statistical power unnecessarily.

title: 5  Nonparametric Tests
text:
5.10 How to Report

5.10.1 Reporting Nonparametric Test Results






Note



In a methods section: “Because [outcome] was markedly non-normal (Shapiro-Wilk p < 0.05; confirmed by QQ-plot), the Wilcoxon rank-sum test was used to compare groups. For comparisons across three or more groups, the Kruskal-Wallis test was used, followed by Dunn’s post-hoc test with Bonferroni correction.”
In results: “Median [outcome] was higher in [group A] than [group B] (median [IQR]: X [L, U] vs Y [L, U]; W = Z, p = P).”
What to always include:

Medians and IQRs (not means and SDs — you chose this test because the mean is inappropriate)
Test statistic (W for Wilcoxon, H for Kruskal-Wallis)
p-value
For Kruskal-Wallis: post-hoc method and correction applied

Internal consistency: If you use a nonparametric test, report medians. Reporting the mean and then quoting a Wilcoxon p-value is internally contradictory.

title: 5  Nonparametric Tests
text:
5.11 Further Reading

Hollander and Wolfe (1999) — Nonparametric Statistical Methods, comprehensive reference
Bland (2015) — Chapters on nonparametric methods in An Introduction to Medical Statistics
?wilcox.test and ?kruskal.test in R: built-in documentation with examples





Bland, Martin. 2015. An Introduction to Medical Statistics. 4th ed. Oxford University Press.


Hollander, Myles, and Douglas A. Wolfe. 1999. Nonparametric Statistical Methods. 2nd ed. John Wiley & Sons.

title: 6  ANOVA: Comparing Means Across Groups
text: 6.1 When Do You Use This?
crumbs: 6  ANOVA: Comparing Means Across Groups

title: 6  ANOVA: Comparing Means Across Groups
text: You have a continuous outcome measured in three or more independent groups and want to know whether the group means differ, without the inflated false-positive rate that comes from running all possible pairwise t-tests. ANOVA tests all groups simultaneously in a single model. For example: comparing a lab value across four disease stages, or comparing cell counts across multiple treatment conditions.
crumbs: 6  ANOVA: Comparing Means Across Groups

title: 6  ANOVA: Comparing Means Across Groups
text:
6.2 Learning Objectives
After completing this session you will be able to:

Explain what ANOVA tests and when it is the right choice
Run a one-way ANOVA in R and interpret the F-statistic and p-value
Conduct post-hoc pairwise comparisons with Tukey’s HSD
Run a two-way ANOVA and interpret a significant interaction term
Check ANOVA assumptions: normality of residuals, homogeneity of variance
crumbs: 6  ANOVA: Comparing Means Across Groups

title: 6  ANOVA: Comparing Means Across Groups
text:
6.3 Background: The Idea Behind ANOVA
A t-test compares two group means. ANOVA (ANalysis Of VAriance) generalises this to \(k\) groups. Despite the name, ANOVA tests means, not variances.
